title: Chapter 24: The Renfield's Choice
content: The apartment had no smell.

This was not an accident. Marcus Webb had never purchased a candle, never cooked a meal that lingered, never brought in flowers or coffee or any of the small combustions that made a place human. The air was conditioned and recycled and nothing. It entered his lungs and left without remark. Five hundred and three years of breathing, and he had nothing to show for it but sterility.

He sat at the kitchen table — white laminate, no tablecloth, no placemat — and the apartment was a white box around him. White walls. White ceiling. White tile floor with grout that had gone grey from neglect, the only blemish in the room, and even that felt accidental, like the building itself had given up trying to be clean on his behalf. There were no decorations. No books. No art. The refrigerator hummed, empty except for a container of dried crickets and a pitcher of tap water. The closets held three identical grey suits and two white shirts. The bathroom had a single towel, a toothbrush, a bar of unscented soap. If Marcus Webb died tonight, the forensic team would conclude the occupant had moved in yesterday and not yet unpacked.
He had lived here for eleven years.

On the table in front of him: the cricket container, lid off, small brown bodies stacked like kindling. And next to it, face-up on the white laminate, his phone. The screen was lit. A podcast app, paused at 04:37 of Episode 3.
*Unfollowed.*
Maya Reyes' voice, frozen mid-syllable.
He had listened to this episode three times. He was about to make it four.

His mind ran in loops. It always had. Serve the master. Protect the master. The master's will is everything. The loops were not thoughts exactly — they were more like the background whir of machinery, a fan running in a room he couldn't enter. They had been there so long that Marcus no longer heard them consciously, the way a man who lives beside a waterfall stops noticing the sound. Serve. Protect. The master's will. They were the architecture of his cognition, the load-bearing walls. Without them, the whole structure would collapse into—
Something else was there now.

Under the loops. Under the fan-noise. A voice, small and persistent, that did not speak in Vlad's vocabulary. It had started two weeks ago, in the parking garage, when Maya Reyes had looked at him with her stupid brave eyes and he had said the words.
*Don't go to Sublevel B.*

He didn't know why he'd said it. The words left his mouth like they'd been ejected from a different body — a body he'd inhabited before, someone who existed prior to the loops, prior to the crickets, prior to five centuries of kneeling. The voice that said *Don't go to Sublevel B* was not the voice that said *Serve the master.* It was louder. Rougher. It had edges.
It sounded like someone who used to have a name.
Marcus pressed play.
"—thinks we're just data points in his immortality algorithm. But he forgot—"
He paused it. Rewound. 04:31.
"—he thinks we're just data points in his immortality algorithm. But he forgot—"
Paused. Rewound. 04:31.
"—he thinks we're just data points in his immortality algorithm. But he forgot — we own the data."

The voice came through the phone's small speaker, tinny and compressed, the way all voices sound through glass and silicon. But Maya's voice had a quality that transcended the medium. Not beauty — conviction. The certainty of someone who had decided that the truth was worth more than her safety. Marcus had never known that kind of certainty. His certainties had been installed, not chosen. The master's will is everything. Serve. Protect. Installed. Like an operating system loaded onto bare hardware.
*We own the data.*
Something moved in his chest.

It was small. A flutter, a displacement, like a bird landing on a branch that hadn't felt weight in so long it had forgotten it was capable of bending. He pressed his palm flat against his sternum, as if he could contain whatever was happening there. The feeling was warm. And it hurt — not the sharp hurt of injury, but the deep ache of something long-compressed beginning to expand into a space that had forgotten it existed. Like a lung reinflating. Like a hand unclenching after half a millennium of making a fist.

He couldn't name it. The vocabulary for what he was feeling had been stripped from him centuries ago, along with everything else. But the feeling didn't need his naming. It was there, warm and aching, and it was his.
**[FORMAT BREAK: THE MIRROR]**
The bathroom.

He didn't decide to go. His legs took him. The white tiled floor was cold under his bare feet — the first tactile sensation he'd registered in hours, maybe days. He had gone numb, the way you go numb when you stop being a person and start being a function. But the cold reached him. The cold said: *you are here, in this body, on this floor.*
The mirror.

He hadn't looked — truly *looked* — at himself in decades. There had been no reason to. The master didn't care about his appearance, only his utility. And Marcus had long since stopped caring about himself, because caring about yourself required a self to care about, and that had been—
He looked.

The face in the mirror was a stranger's. Hollow eyes, set deep in sockets that had collapsed inward over centuries of insufficient feeding. Cheekbones that cut like blades beneath skin that was too thin, too pale, stretched over a skull that had slowly consumed the flesh that once covered it. His mouth was a thin line, lips pressed together, corners turned down — the mouth of a man who had forgotten that mouths could do anything except eat insects and deliver reports.

But hollow eyes weren't always hollow. He could almost see it — a ghost-image overlaid on the mirror, like a double exposure. Rounder cheeks. Eyes that held light instead of consuming it. A mouth that curved. Did he smile? Once? The memory was there, but it was underwater, fifty fathoms down, and he was reaching for it with arms that had forgotten how to swim.

He touched the glass. Cool and smooth under his fingertips. His reflection touched its own face in the same instant, and the gesture felt like greeting — *hello, I'm still here, barely, but here* — and also like mourning — *hello, I'm still here, and I shouldn't be, not like this.*
Who was he before?
He was Marcus Webb. No. Marcus Webb was the name Vlad gave him, carved into his identity like a brand, overwriting whatever had been there before. His real name was—
He couldn't remember.

He had been *Marcus* for so long that the original had been paved over. Buried under centuries of service and crickets and obedience. But the *feeling* was there. The voice that said *Don't go to Sublevel B* — that voice had a name. It had a life. It had a sister, once, whose face looked out from the only personal artifact in this white box of an apartment, and she died in 1987, and he could barely remember her name either, and that was the worst part, that was the ache that made his hand press harder against the mirror, that was the warm thing in his chest sharpening into something with teeth, because how do you forget your own sister? How do you live for five hundred years and let the only person who loved you before the master dissolve into static?
The voice in his chest had a name. He couldn't remember it. But it was there.
**[END FORMAT BREAK]**
He went back to the table. The phone. The crickets. The white walls pressing in, but differently now — not the comfortable enclosure of a cage but the suffocation of a coffin.

He picked up the phone. The weight of it in his hand was real. The glass was warm from his palm, from the battery, from the small constant heat of a device that was more alive than he'd been in centuries.
He opened a new message.
To: Maya Reyes.

His thumbs moved over the keyboard. The words assembled themselves on the screen, green text bubbles forming like cells dividing, a message creating itself from the part of him that had spoken in the parking garage:
*The kill switch at the NYSE podium is behind the bell. There's a panel on the left side. Red button. When Vlad reaches for it — and he will — you have three seconds to stop him.*
He stared at it.
His thumb hovered over the send arrow. A blue circle. So small. So much weight contained in a circle no wider than a fingertip.

If Vlad discovered this, the punishment would be worse than death. Marcus had seen what happened to those who betrayed the master. In 1678, a thrall named Søren had attempted to leave. Vlad had let him run for three days — Marcus knew it was three days because Vlad had made him count — before bringing him back. Søren's skin was kept in the east wing. He was still alive in it. That was the point. Not death. *Continuance.* Søren had been screaming for three hundred and forty-eight years.

Marcus knew this. He knew it the way he knew the loops — *serve, protect, the master's will* — the way he knew the taste of crickets, dry and papery, the way he knew the white walls and the absent smell and the numbness. He knew the cost of defiance with the same certainty he knew the cost of breathing.
But.

He heard Maya's voice again. Not from the phone this time. From memory. *We own the data.* And underneath her voice, his own — the one that said *Don't go to Sublevel B.* The one that had edges. The one that remembered, even if it couldn't name what it remembered, a time before Moscow, before the Turn, before five centuries of eating insects and kneeling and serving a monster who had taken everything from him including the knowledge of what had been taken.
He pressed send.

The whoosh sound. Gone. The message was no longer his. It was hers. It was the world's. It was out, like a bird from a cage, and it would never come back, and whatever happened next, this moment — the press of his thumb against that blue circle — this was the first choice Marcus had made in five hundred and three years that was not the master's will.
This was his.
He put the phone down. The screen dimmed, then went dark, and the apartment returned to its default state: white, silent, featureless. A box for a thing that wasn't quite a person.

He reached into the cricket container. His fingers found one — dry, light, the papery shell giving slightly under pressure. Six jointed legs twitched against his skin. He brought it up, held it between thumb and forefinger, at eye level. The cricket's head was small, black, compound. It looked at him with the blank attention of something that had never questioned why it existed.
He looked at it.

He had eaten ten thousand of these. A hundred thousand. More. They were fuel. They were what the master permitted him, the minimum caloric input required to maintain a body that served a function. He had never considered them as creatures. He had never considered them at all.
He brought the cricket toward his mouth. Stopped.
The legs moved against his fingers. Tiny. Alive. Persisting.
He put it down on the table.

The cricket sat there on the white laminate, motionless for a moment, then twitched its antennae and took a hesitant step. It didn't know where it was. It didn't know it had been spared. It was just a cricket, doing what crickets do, which is to be small and continue.

Marcus stared at it. Then at the bathroom mirror, visible through the open door, his hollow reflection staring back. Then at the photograph on the wall — the only object in this apartment that he had chosen, that he had kept, that existed because he needed it to exist even though he could no longer remember why. A woman. His sister. A face that had once been the center of a world that wasn't white and wasn't sterile and wasn't defined by the master's will.
He opened his mouth. No sound at first. Just breath, the recycled air of the apartment passing through vocal cords that had forgotten how to shape anything except reports and obedience. Then:
"Willehm."

The whisper was barely audible. A breath with consonants. A name — not Marcus. Something older. Something Dutch, from a language that hadn't been spoken in four hundred years, from a village that no longer existed, from a man who had stood in a field in 1523 and looked at the sky and had a name that was his own.
*Willehm.*
The Renfield chose.

He stood. His coat was on the hook by the door — grey, like everything else, but warmer than the rest of this place. He put it on. The fabric settled on his shoulders, and for the first time in memory, it felt like armor instead of a uniform.
He opened the door.

The hallway was long and fluorescent and led to an elevator that led to a lobby that led to a street that led to a city that led to a world, and he didn't know where he was going in it. He didn't have a plan. He didn't have a destination. He had a coat and a name he could barely pronounce and a message he couldn't take back and a cricket he didn't eat and a feeling in his chest that was warm and aching and his, *his*, not the master's, not the loops', not the architecture of someone else's will — *his.*
He stepped into the hallway. The door closed behind him.
He was not going back to the master. Not tonight.

And somewhere across the city, in a white apartment that had no smell, a cricket walked across a kitchen table, alive, because for the first time in five hundred years, someone had decided not to consume it.

And somewhere deeper — in a sublevel, in a boardroom, in a place where algorithms mapped the architecture of immortality — something shifted. A data point moved. A variable changed. And the master, who had never in five centuries had reason to watch his own Renfield, did not notice.
Not yet.
